text: Prescribing exercise intensity based on a fixed percentage of maximal anchors (VO2max, peak VO2, HRmax, maximum work rate) has little merit for eliciting distinct or domain-specific homeostatic perturbations.
The first lactate threshold (LT1), gas exchange threshold (GET), and ventilatory threshold (VT) are valid submaximal anchors for delineating the moderate and heavy exercise intensity domains.
Most commonly used submaximal threshold methods, with the exception of critical power (CP) and critical speed (CS), lack evidence to validly delineate the heavy and severe exercise intensity domains.
Critical power (CP) and critical speed (CS) are the most valid submaximal anchors for delineating the boundary between the heavy and severe exercise intensity domains.
Exercise intensity is best understood through three physiological domains — moderate, heavy, and severe — each characterized by distinct oxygen uptake kinetics and blood lactate responses.
Acute physiological responses to exercise at a given intensity are not always predictive of chronic training adaptations, so training studies are needed to validate intensity prescription methods.
